passages.text: Hi Jisoo, I hope you're doing well. I wanted to remind you about our group project meeting tomorrow at 3 PM in the school library. Please bring any research materials you have. Let's make sure we're all prepared to discuss our parts. See you then!
passages.teachPoint: Practicing reminders and planning for group activities.
passages.question: What time is the group project meeting scheduled for?

passages.text: Dear Mom, I just wanted to let you know that I arrived safely at the school camp. The bus ride was long, but I enjoyed it because I sat with my friends. We'll have various activities over the next few days. I'll call you soon. Love, Jin.
passages.teachPoint: Writing updates about travel and reassuring parents.
passages.question: Who did Jin sit with on the bus ride?

passages.text: Hello Mr. Kim, I hope this email finds you well. I am writing to request an extension for my history project. Due to some personal issues, I haven't been able to complete it on time. I would really appreciate your understanding. Thank you.
passages.teachPoint: How to formally request an extension for assignments.
passages.question: What is the student requesting from Mr. Kim?

passages.text: Hi everyone, as a reminder, our school's annual sports day is on Friday. Please make sure to wear your team colors and bring your own water bottles. Let's show our team spirit and have a fun day together!
passages.teachPoint: Informing about school events and necessary preparations.
passages.question: What should students bring to the sports day?

passages.text: Dear Alex, I hope you're having a great week. I just finished reading the book you recommended. It was amazing! The storyline was captivating, and I couldn't put it down. Thank you for suggesting it. Let's discuss it over coffee soon.
passages.teachPoint: Expressing gratitude and suggesting a meetup to discuss a book.
passages.question: What did the writer think about the book Alex recommended?

teachPoints: Practicing reminders and planning for group activities.
Writing updates about travel and reassuring parents.
How to formally request an extension for assignments.
Informing about school events and necessary preparations.
Expressing gratitude and suggesting a meetup to discuss a book.
questions: What time is the group project meeting scheduled for?
Who did Jin sit with on the bus ride?
What is the student requesting from Mr. Kim?
What should students bring to the sports day?
What did the writer think about the book Alex recommended?

script: A: Hey, do you have any plans for the weekend?
B: Not really, I was thinking about just staying in. Why?
A: I heard there's a new art exhibit opening downtown. Do you want to check it out?
B: That sounds interesting! What time were you thinking of going?
A: How about Saturday afternoon, around 2 PM?
B: That works for me. Should we invite anyone else?
A: Sure, let's ask Clara and Daniel. They might be interested too.
items.question: What are the speakers planning to do this weekend?

script: A: Did you finish the report for the meeting tomorrow?
B: Not yet, I'm still working on the last section.
A: Do you need any help? I have some free time this afternoon.
B: That would be great, actually. I'm having trouble with the data analysis part.
A: No problem, I can help you with that. Let's meet in the conference room at 3 PM.
B: Sounds good, I'll bring my laptop and notes.
A: Perfect, see you then.
items.question: What time will they meet to work on the report?

script: A: I'm thinking about getting a new phone. My current one is too slow.
B: Have you decided which model you want?
A: Not yet, I'm considering either the latest iPhone or the new Samsung Galaxy.
B: Both are good choices. What features are most important to you?
A: I really want a good camera and long battery life.
B: In that case, you should compare their specs online. It might help you decide.
A: That's a good idea. I'll do that tonight.
items.question: What is the main reason A wants a new phone?
choices: A. The current phone's screen is cracked
B. The current phone is too slow
C. The current phone's battery is perfect
D. A wants to try a different brand
E. The current phone is too big

script: A: Hi, can I get a large coffee and a blueberry muffin, please?
B: Sure, would you like any milk or sugar with your coffee?
A: Just a little bit of milk, no sugar.
B: Alright, anything else for you today?
A: No, that's all. How much do I owe you?
B: That'll be $5.50, please.
A: Here you go. Thank you!
items.question: What did the customer order?
choices: A. A small coffee and a croissant
B. A large coffee and a blueberry muffin
C. A medium coffee and a doughnut
D. A large tea and a chocolate chip cookie
E. A small latte and a scone

script: A: Are you going to the company picnic this weekend?
B: I wasn't planning to, but I heard it's going to be a lot of fun.
A: Yeah, they're having a barbecue and some games for families.
B: That sounds nice. Do you know what time it starts?
A: It starts at noon and goes until 5 PM.
B: Maybe I'll drop by for a little while. Are you bringing your family?
A: Yes, we all love these kinds of events.
items.question: What event are the speakers discussing?

script: A: Have you seen the new movie that just came out?
B: Not yet, I heard it's really good though.
A: I watched it last night, and it was amazing!
B: Really? What did you like about it?
A: The storyline was very engaging and the special effects were fantastic.
B: I definitely need to check it out. Do you want to watch it again sometime?
A: Sure, I'd love to see it again.
items.question: What did A like about the movie?
choices: B. The storyline and special effects
C. The ending and the director
E. The actors and the script

script: A: I heard you started a new job recently. How's it going?
B: It's going well, thanks. I'm still getting used to the new environment.
A: That's understandable. What do you do there?
B: I'm working as a project manager in their IT department.
A: That sounds interesting. What's the best part about your job?
B: I really enjoy collaborating with different teams and learning new things.
A: That's great! I'm sure you'll do well there.
items.question: What is B's new job title?

script: A: Do you want to grab lunch together today?
B: I'd love to, but I have a meeting at noon.
A: How about after your meeting? We could go around 1 PM.
B: That works for me. Where do you want to eat?
A: There's a new Italian place that just opened. We could try that.
B: Sounds good, I've heard their pasta is delicious.
A: Great, I'll meet you at the office lobby at 1 PM.
items.question: What time will they meet for lunch?

script: A: I need to buy a gift for my sister's birthday. Any ideas?
B: How about a nice piece of jewelry?
A: That's a good idea. She loves necklaces.
B: There's a great jewelry store at the mall. You should check it out.
A: I think I will. Do you know if they have any sales going on?
B: Yes, they have a 20% discount on selected items this week.
A: Perfect, I'll go there this afternoon.
items.question: What gift is A considering for their sister?

script: A: Can you recommend a good restaurant in town?
B: Sure, what type of food are you in the mood for?
A: I'm thinking about trying some Mexican food.
B: There's a place called 'El Sol' that's really good. It's just a few blocks from here.
A: That sounds perfect. Do they have vegetarian options?
B: Yes, they have a great selection of vegetarian dishes.
A: Thanks for the recommendation! I'll check it out.
items.question: What type of restaurant is A looking for?

words.meaning: to obtain something from a specified source
words.alt_meaning: to deduce or infer from data or information
words.example_sentence: Scientists can derive the age of ancient artifacts based on carbon dating.
words.context_explain: In scientific and logical contexts, deriving often involves using data or information to reach a conclusion or obtain results.

words.meaning: to limit or restrict someone or something
words.alt_meaning: to compel or force toward a particular course of action
words.example_sentence: Economic factors can constrain the growth of new businesses.
words.context_explain: In logical and analytical contexts, constraints are often conditions or limitations that must be considered.

words.meaning: to show or prove to be right or reasonable
words.alt_meaning: to provide valid reasons or evidence in support of something
words.example_sentence: The lawyer had to justify his client's actions to the court.
words.context_explain: In debates or arguments, justifying involves providing evidence or reasoning to support a claim.

words.meaning: to explain the meaning of information or actions
words.alt_meaning: to understand or perceive in a particular way
words.example_sentence: Historians often need to interpret ancient texts to uncover their full meanings.
words.context_explain: Interpretation in logical contexts involves analyzing data or information to derive meaning or understanding.

words.meaning: to deduce or conclude information from evidence and reasoning
words.alt_meaning: to derive as a conclusion from facts or premises
words.example_sentence: From the data gathered, researchers can infer patterns of behavior.
words.context_explain: In logical reasoning, inferring involves drawing conclusions based on available evidence and logical processes.

words.meaning: to come close to a value or quantity but not be exact
words.alt_meaning: to estimate or bring near to correctness
words.example_sentence: The engineer had to approximate the amount of material needed for the project.
words.context_explain: Approximating in mathematical and scientific contexts involves estimating values that are close to the actual or desired result.

words.meaning: to regard something as being caused by
words.alt_meaning: to assign a characteristic or quality to someone or something
words.example_sentence: The success of the project was largely attributed to the team's hard work.
words.context_explain: In logical and analytical contexts, attributing involves identifying the cause or source of an outcome or phenomenon.

words.meaning: to control or maintain the rate or speed of a process
words.alt_meaning: to set or adjust the rules or parameters
words.example_sentence: The government has the authority to regulate industries for public safety.
words.context_explain: Regulation in scientific and policy contexts involves controlling or directing processes according to specific standards or laws.

words.meaning: to strengthen or support an idea, habit, or structure
words.alt_meaning: to make something more pronounced or intense
words.example_sentence: Positive feedback can reinforce good behavior in students.
words.context_explain: Reinforcement in psychological and educational contexts involves strengthening behaviors or concepts through positive or negative feedback.

words.meaning: to support, maintain, or endure over time
words.alt_meaning: to provide the necessities for life or existence
words.example_sentence: Renewable energy sources are essential to sustain the environment.
words.context_explain: In environmental and economic contexts, sustaining involves maintaining conditions or resources over a prolonged period.

words.meaning: to make an action or process easier or more achievable
words.alt_meaning: to assist or help in the progress of something
words.example_sentence: The new software will facilitate the management of customer data.
words.context_explain: Facilitation in organizational and educational contexts involves making processes or activities easier to achieve or manage.

words.meaning: to hinder, restrain, or prevent an action or process
words.alt_meaning: to suppress or hold back
words.example_sentence: Excessive regulations can inhibit innovation in technology.
words.context_explain: Inhibition in biological and psychological contexts involves processes or actions being slowed down or prevented.

words.meaning: to make up for a deficiency or loss
words.alt_meaning: to provide something good in place of something bad
words.example_sentence: The company offered to compensate the customers for the inconvenience caused.
words.context_explain: Compensating in economic and social contexts involves providing a benefit or reparation for a deficit or loss.

words.meaning: to confirm the truth or accuracy of something
words.alt_meaning: to substantiate or verify as legitimate
words.example_sentence: The results of the study were validated through peer review.
words.context_explain: Validation in scientific and research contexts involves confirming that data or results are accurate and reliable.

words.meaning: to arrange in a straight line or in correct relative positions
words.alt_meaning: to bring into cooperation or agreement
words.example_sentence: The company's goals should align with its mission statement.
words.context_explain: Alignment in organizational contexts involves ensuring that strategies or objectives are in agreement with overarching goals.

words.meaning: to make something as effective or functional as possible
words.alt_meaning: to improve or enhance efficiency
words.example_sentence: The software was optimized to run faster on older computers.
words.context_explain: Optimization in technological and operational contexts involves making systems or processes work at their best potential.

words.meaning: to combine various elements into a coherent whole
words.alt_meaning: to produce by chemical synthesis
words.example_sentence: The scientist synthesized a new compound in the lab.
words.context_explain: Synthesis in academic and scientific contexts involves merging different ideas or components to create a new and comprehensive understanding or product.

words.meaning: to have a mutual relationship or connection
words.alt_meaning: to show a relationship between two variables or sets of data
words.example_sentence: The study found that smoking correlates with an increased risk of heart disease.
words.context_explain: Correlation in statistical and research contexts involves identifying and assessing relationships between variables or data sets.

words.meaning: to express an idea or feeling fluently and coherently
words.alt_meaning: to pronounce clearly and distinctly
words.example_sentence: She was able to articulate her thoughts during the presentation.
words.context_explain: Articulation in communication contexts involves clearly expressing thoughts, ideas, or arguments in a manner that is easily understood.

words.meaning: to extend or project known information into an unknown area
words.alt_meaning: to estimate or predict based on existing data
words.example_sentence: Scientists can extrapolate future climate patterns based on historical data.
words.context_explain: Extrapolation in scientific and statistical contexts involves using known information to make predictions about unknown or future data.

words.meaning: to express or measure the quantity of something
words.alt_meaning: to determine the amount or number of something
words.example_sentence: Researchers quantified the amount of carbon emissions produced by the factory.
words.context_explain: Quantification in scientific and analytical contexts involves measuring or expressing something in numerical terms.

words.meaning: to recognize or ascertain what makes someone or something different
words.alt_meaning: to identify differences between items or concepts
words.example_sentence: It's important to differentiate between fact and opinion in any argument.
words.context_explain: Differentiation in educational and logical contexts involves identifying differences between concepts, ideas, or entities.

words.meaning: to combine or incorporate parts into a whole
words.alt_meaning: to unite with something else
words.example_sentence: The new policy aims to integrate technology into the classroom.
words.context_explain: Integration in organizational and technical contexts involves combining different components or systems to function together as a cohesive unit.

words.meaning: to expect or predict
words.alt_meaning: to look forward to with pleasure
words.example_sentence: The company needs to anticipate market trends to remain competitive.
words.context_explain: Anticipation in strategic and planning contexts involves predicting or expecting future events or trends based on current information.

words.meaning: to make something clear; to explain
words.alt_meaning: to shed light on a subject
words.example_sentence: The professor elucidated the complex theories in a way that was easy to understand.
words.context_explain: Elucidation in academic and educational contexts involves clarifying complex ideas or theories to enhance understanding.

words.meaning: to check or prove the validity or accuracy of something
words.alt_meaning: to authorize or confirm as official or legitimate
words.example_sentence: The results of the experiment need to be validated by further research.
words.context_explain: Validation in scientific and research contexts involves confirming that results or data are accurate and reliable through additional experiments or analysis.

questions.question: What does 'inhibit' mean in a logical context?
What does 'optimize' entail?
choices: To make as effective as possible
questions.answer: To make as effective as possible

questions.question: What does the term 'synthesize' refer to?
What does 'facilitate' mean in an educational context?
What does 'elucidate' mean in an academic setting?
